ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Kung Pao crispy enoki mushrooms specifically; closest parent 'mushroom food product' (FOODON_03311737) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Mushrooms and other fungi - cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for battered/fried enoki mushrooms in Kung Pao sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific prepared mushroom dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Zi Char shared serving of Spicy Kung Pao Crispy Enoki Mushrooms is approximately 200 g, representing one plate shared among 2–3 diners as part of a multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central — Mushrooms, enoki, raw (FDC ID 168580); Battered and deep-fried coating adjustment; Kung Pao sauce components (soy sauce, sugar, chilli, sesame oil) per USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Spicy Kung Pao Crispy Enoki Mushrooms exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated by component analysis: raw enoki mushrooms (USDA FDC 168580) as base, adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption (~8–10 g fat per 100 g fried product), batter addition (~15 g carbohydrate per 100 g), and Kung Pao sauce contribution (soy sauce sodium, sugar, sesame oil). Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed mushroom and battered fried vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on battered fried vegetables (GI ~45–55) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Kung Pao Crispy Enoki Mushrooms. GI estimated at 48 based on analogous battered and deep-fried vegetable/mushroom dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. Enoki mushrooms have low intrinsic GI; batter and sauce add moderate glycemic carbohydrates. Composite GI of 48 is defensible as low-GI. GL per serving = (48 × 20.5 × 200/100) / 100 = 19.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced soy sauce, as a full 200 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium — nearly 37% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~19.7 per serving) from batter carbohydrates and added sugar in Kung Pao sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the batter coating and sugar in the Kung Pao sauce contribute a moderate glycemic load of approximately 19.7 g per serving; pairing with high-fibre vegetables and limiting portion to 100 g can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat from deep-frying (~11.5 g per 100 g) with relatively low saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g); sodium load is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the deep-fried preparation adds moderate fat (predominantly unsaturated from vegetable oil), but the high sodium from soy sauce is the primary concern; choosing a smaller portion and requesting low-sodium soy sauce is advisable to reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Kung Pao Crispy Enoki Mushrooms is a modern Zi Char dish popular in Malaysian and Singaporean Chinese restaurants, featuring enoki mushrooms (Flammulina velutipes — also known as golden needle mushrooms or 金针菇) that are coated in a light batter of wheat flour and cornstarch, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The fried mushrooms are then tossed in a bold Kung Pao sauce made from dried red chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and sesame oil, and finished with roasted peanuts and spring onions. The dish delivers a complex flavour profile: spicy, savoury, slightly sweet, and tangy, with a satisfying crunch from the batter contrasting against the tender mushroom strands inside. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~198 kcal per 100 g) primarily from carbohydrates in the batter and fat from deep-frying. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce, making it a moderate concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate (~19.7 per 200 g serving) owing to batter carbohydrates and added sugar. The dish is vegetarian and vegan-friendly, and is typically served as a shared side dish in a Zi Char meal. Enoki mushrooms contribute dietary fibre, B vitamins (riboflavin, niacin), and potassium. This dish is a creative fusion of Sichuan-inspired Kung Pao flavours with the crispy-fried mushroom trend that has gained popularity across Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char stalls and seafood restaurants.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Spicy Kung Pao Crispy Enoki Mushrooms exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for raw enoki mushrooms and standard deep-frying and sauce adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from battered fried vegetable data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis (USDA FDC enoki mushroom base + deep-frying oil absorption + batter carbohydrate + Kung Pao sauce contributions); no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as vegetable cooking oils used in Zi Char deep-frying contain negligible industrial trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central vegetable oil entries. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as enoki mushrooms contain negligible preformed vitamin A and the sauce components do not contribute significant beta-carotene; not reported in the primary source. 6. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as not separately reported in the USDA enoki mushroom entry used for estimation. 7. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as not separately reported in the component estimation sources. 8. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as not separately reported in the component estimation sources. 9. selenium_mcg is -1 as not separately reported in the component estimation sources. 10. GI set to 48 (low) based on analogous battered fried vegetable/mushroom dishes from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); gi_category is low per ≤55 threshold. 11. GL per serving = (48 × 20.5 × 200/100) / 100 = 19.7 g. 12. Food name corrected from input 'Spicy Kung Paocrispy Enoki-Mushrooms' to canonical form 'Spicy Kung Pao Crispy Enoki Mushrooms'. 13. Cuisine tagged as Malaysian/Zi Char; multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.
